does: This document presents the system card for GPT-5.2, OpenAI's latest model in the GPT-5 series. It documents the safety mitigation strategies, training methodology, and risk assessment framework applied to these models. The work describes how GPT-5.2 was trained on diverse data sources including public internet information, third-party partnerships, and user-generated or human trainer-provided data. The safety approach largely maintains continuity with previous GPT-5 and GPT-5.1 system cards, suggesting incremental refinement rather than fundamental methodological shifts. The system card serves as a comprehensive transparency document detailing both the model's capabilities and the deliberate safety measures implemented throughout its development and deployment.
connects: This work builds directly on the established practice of publishing system cards for large language models, extending the documentation framework first introduced with earlier GPT models and formalized in OpenAI's GPT-5 system card. It responds to growing regulatory and community expectations for AI safety transparency, continuing the lineage of responsible disclosure practices in the field. The work also connects to broader trends in AI safety research around red-teaming, adversarial testing, and mitigation strategy development that have evolved across the ML community over recent years. By maintaining consistency with prior system cards while documenting updates, it reflects the iterative approach to safety research where lessons learned from earlier deployments inform subsequent model releases.
enables: This documentation enables stakeholders—including policymakers, researchers, and the public—to understand the safety considerations embedded in state-of-the-art models. By establishing clear documentation standards and transparency practices, it facilitates external auditing and accountability mechanisms for frontier AI systems. The system card approach enables future work in AI safety evaluation by creating benchmarks and baselines for comparing mitigation strategies across model versions. It also supports the development of industry standards for responsible AI deployment, potentially influencing how other labs document and communicate about their own large-scale models. Furthermore, this transparency work enables researchers to identify remaining gaps in safety approaches and propose new mitigation strategies for subsequent generations.
